critique.summary: OpenTTD will no longer be available as a standalone game on Steam but can be obtained as part of a bundle with the re-released Transport Tycoon Deluxe. The game remains free, but new players must purchase the bundle to access OpenTTD on Steam.
mainPoints: OpenTTD is now available only as part of a bundle with Transport Tycoon Deluxe on Steam.
Existing owners of OpenTTD will not be affected by this change.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the changes to the distribution of OpenTTD on Steam and clarifies that the game remains free, albeit with a new purchasing requirement for new players. It provides clear information about the bundle and reassures current players of continued support.
considerations: The article does not mention how this change may affect the player base or community engagement.
There is no discussion on the implications for future updates or features in OpenTTD.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the implications of Atari's changes to the distribution of OpenTTD on Steam, with users expressing varied opinions on Atari's actions and the value of OpenTTD compared to the original TTD. Some comments highlight concerns about the fairness of Atari's monetization strategy, while others appreciate the potential benefits of the arrangement.

comments.author: ndiddy
comments.text: Obviously having OpenTTD available for free on Steam would jeopardize Atari's paid rerelease of Transport Tycoon Deluxe...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a reasonable perspective on the business implications of OpenTTD's availability and suggests a fair compromise.

comments.author: TuxMark5
comments.text: This doesn't feel right for me. OpenTTD is so much superior in every way compared to the original TTD...
comments.analysis: While the comment expresses strong feelings about the situation, it lacks concrete evidence to support the claims about Atari's actions.

comments.author: nout
comments.text: What is the story with OpenGFX then? It sounds like OpenTTD is completely new codebase and OpenGFX...
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid question about the relationship between OpenTTD and OpenGFX, contributing to the discussion without strong bias.

comments.author: singron
comments.text: If you like OpenTTD, you may want to try OpenTTD-JGRPP (JGRennison's Patch Pack)...
comments.analysis: The suggestion to explore additional features in OpenTTD is helpful, but it diverts from the main discussion about Atari's distribution changes.

comments.author: r2vcap
comments.text: Atari? I never expected to see that ancient name again. If I remember correctly, I've been playing OpenTTD...
comments.analysis: This comment reflects on personal experience with OpenTTD and expresses skepticism about Atari's involvement, though it lacks depth.

comments.author: Bengalilol
comments.text: Surprisingly, this is an interesting outcome. Atari could have been worse (better too).
comments.analysis: The comment provides a neutral take on the situation but lacks specific insights or arguments to support its viewpoint.

comments.author: stephbook
comments.text: Honestly fair and understandable. OpenTTD builds on TTD (it's right there in the name.)...
comments.analysis: While the comment acknowledges the relationship between OpenTTD and TTD, it does not engage deeply with the implications of Atari's actions.

comments.author: ocdtrekkie
comments.text: This seems quite positive to me: Clearly the rightsholders are not being total jerks since they're happy to allow an OpenTTD bundle...
comments.analysis: The comment offers an optimistic view of the situation but may overlook potential issues with Atari's monetization strategy.

comments.author: xyzzy_plugh
comments.text: It would be nice if someone could provide some explanation as to why this situation is necessary...
comments.analysis: This comment expresses curiosity about the situation but does not contribute significantly to the ongoing discussion.

comments.author: khoirul
comments.text: Lots of words, but nowhere does it say: why? Did Chris Sawyer sue them or something?
comments.analysis: The comment's dismissive tone detracts from constructive dialogue and does not provide a meaningful contribution to the discussion.

comments.author: binaryturtle
comments.text: So does OpenTTD gets a part of the generated income then? The team has costs too to support the game.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a pertinent question about revenue sharing but lacks further exploration of the implications.

comments.author: sarru_kin
comments.analysis: The suggestion to use a different platform does not address the core issue and is not constructive.

comments.author: basilikum
comments.text: OpenTTD with its beyond clunky UI has a special spot in my heart...
comments.analysis: While nostalgic, this comment does not engage with the current topic and lacks relevance.

comments.author: anesxvito
comments.text: Interesting to see an open-source game navigating Steam distribution...
comments.analysis: The comment introduces an interesting perspective but does not delve into the implications of Atari's actions.

comments.author: xyzzy_plugh
comments.text: Weirdly the OpenTTD Steam page seems to be missing entirely at the moment...
comments.analysis: This observation is relevant but does not contribute significantly to the broader discussion.

comments.author: boxedemp
comments.text: I get it, but it makes me sad. At least I already have it in my library...
comments.analysis: The emotional response adds a personal touch but lacks analytical depth regarding the situation.

comments.author: jjmarr
comments.text: I like Simutrans more because the cargo and passengers have destinations in mind...
comments.analysis: While the comment shares a personal preference, it strays from the main topic and does not address Atari's actions.

comments.author: ocdtrekkie
comments.text: This seems quite positive to me: Clearly the rightsholders are not being total jerks since they’re happy to allow an OpenTTD bundle...
comments.analysis: The comment offers an optimistic view of the situation but may overlook potential issues with Atari's monetization strategy.
